introSection: Python was a steel roller coaster built by American firm Arrow Development previously located at Busch Gardens Africa in Tampa, Florida, USA. It opened in 1976 and closed in 2006.

sections.title: History
sections.content:
Python opened on July 1, 1976. It was the park's first roller coaster, as well as Florida's first roller coaster to go upside down.

The ride was repainted in 2003 and the trains were also painted with the park's current logo, switching from the classic Python logo.

Python closed on October 31, 2006. Demolition began in November 2006. The removal of Python was necessary to make way for the park's Jungala attraction, and was part of the largest renovation in Busch Gardens' history. Along with Python, the area's Tiger's Den gift shop, and Python Soft Serve have been torn down as a part of the Congo renovation. While Python was demolished, the trains were sent to Busch Gardens Williamsburg to be used as spare parts for Loch Ness Monster.

sections.title: Incident
sections.content: On July 10, 1976, a newspaper reporter died after riding the Python. It was believed he suffered a heart attack.

fullText:
Python was a steel roller coaster built by American firm Arrow Development previously located at Busch Gardens Africa in Tampa, Florida, USA. It opened in 1976 and closed in 2006.

Python opened on July 1, 1976. It was the park's first roller coaster, as well as Florida's first roller coaster to go upside down.

The ride was repainted in 2003 and the trains were also painted with the park's current logo, switching from the classic Python logo.

Python closed on October 31, 2006. Demolition began in November 2006. The removal of Python was necessary to make way for the park's Jungala attraction, and was part of the largest renovation in Busch Gardens' history. Along with Python, the area's Tiger's Den gift shop, and Python Soft Serve have been torn down as a part of the Congo renovation. While Python was demolished, the trains were sent to Busch Gardens Williamsburg to be used as spare parts for Loch Ness Monster.



Yellow track and black supports.



On July 10, 1976, a newspaper reporter died after riding the Python. It was believed he suffered a heart attack.
